elements.sent-2411-001: We present a generative modeling approach based on the variational inference framework for likelihood-free simulation-based inference.
elements.sent-2411-002: The method leverages latent variables within variational autoencoders to efficiently estimate complex posterior distributions arising from stochastic simulations.
elements.sent-2411-003: We explore two variations of this approach distinguished by their treatment of the prior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-004: The first model adapts the prior based on observed data using a multivariate prior network, enhancing generalization across various posterior queries.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast, the second model utilizes a standard Gaussian prior, offering simplicity while still effectively capturing complex posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate the efficacy of these models on well-established benchmark problems, achieving results comparable to flow-based approaches while maintaining computational efficiency and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-007: The simulation-based inference (SBI) problem involves statistical inference of parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) of a stochastic simulation model \( M(\boldsymbol{\theta , \xi) \) containing random states \(\xi\), from observed data \(\boldsymbol{y}\) \citep{Cranmer20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Once calibrated, the simulation model can be used to reason about, analyze and explain the observed data \(\boldsymbol{y}\) in the context of the corresponding physical process \citep{Lavin2021SimulationIT .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Several challenges arise in SBI owing to model stochasticity and potential multi-valuedness, where different sets of parameter values can produce similar observations, or similar parameters may lead to varied outputs.
elements.sent-2411-010: Consequently, the task is to infer the posterior distribution \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y ) \) in a Bayesian setting.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: A user-defined prior distribution \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta ) \) encodes domain knowledge or assumptions about plausible parameter values.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The likelihood function \( p(\mathbf{y \mid \boldsymbol{\theta ) \) is however, often intractable due to intricate stochastic dynamics, high-dimensional integration, or latent variables making analytical computation impractical \citep{sisson2018handbook .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Although a closed-form expression for the likelihood function \( p(\mathbf{y \mid \boldsymbol{\theta ) \) is unavailable, samples from this distribution can be generated by evaluating the simulator \( M \) with varying \( \xi \) for different \( \boldsymbol{\theta \) drawn from the prior, effectively yielding samples from the joint distribution \( (\boldsymbol{\theta , \mathbf{y ) \sim p(\boldsymbol{\theta , \mathbf{y ) \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Deep learning based approaches that directly approximate the model likelihood, or the posterior distribution have gained increasing popularity in the SBI setting \citep{zammit2024neural .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Several approaches involve flow-based models, which are potentially compute-intensive and also restrict the design space of neural networks to invertible functions with computationally efficient Jacobian calculations \citep{flow .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This constraint can limit the flexibility of the model in handling more complex data structures.
elements.sent-2411-017: Alternative approaches like Generative Adversarial Training for SBI (GATSBI) \citep{Ramesh22 use an adversarial network, where a generator produces simulations that mimic observed data, and a discriminator distinguishes between real and simulated data.}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the adversarial approach can suffer from training instability, mode collapse, and difficulties in balancing the generator and discriminator networks \citep{Arjovsky17a, Arjovsky17b , complicating training and limiting robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-019: As VAEs are not constrained by the invertibility requirement, they allow seamless integration of various neural network architectures without significant modifications as opposed to flow-based models.
elements.sent-2411-020: VAEs also avoid the instability associated with GANs due to adversarial learning dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-021: As a result, C-VAEs can handle complex data structures and dependencies more effectively, making them suitable for a wider range of tasks without requiring external summary networks \citep{Cranmer20 .}

elements.sent-2411-025: A comprehensive comparison on benchmark test problems is presented in Section \ref{Experiments and in the Appendix.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Section \ref{Discussion discusses the results, while Section \ref{Conclusion concludes the paper by summarizing the key findings and further advancements.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: A vast majority of SBI approaches based on deep learning aim to either approximate the likelihood \citep{hermans2020likelihoodfree , or focus on directly approximating the posterior \citep{papamakarios2016fast, NIPS2017_addfa9b7, Greenberg19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Additionally, there exist methods that target both the likelihood and posterior simultaneously \citep{Radev23, glöckler2022variational, wiqvist2021sequential Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE) \citep{papamakarios2016fast was one of the earliest approaches towards one-step posterior estimation, introducing the use of conditional normalizing flows to approximate the posterior distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: By incorporating techniques such as Neural Spline Flows (NSFs) and Mixture Density Networks (MDNs), NPE enhanced both the accuracy and scalability of simulation-based inference.
elements.sent-2411-030: In comparison to traditional approaches like Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) or Sequential Monte-Carlo (SMC)-ABC \citep{sisson2018handbook that rely on repeated simulations and rejection-based schemes, NPE and its derivatives such as Robust NPE \citep{Ward22 are more efficient and flexible.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Sequential Neural Posterior Estimation (SNPE) improves NPE's efficiency by employing sequential training, where initial parameters are sampled from the prior, and the model is iteratively refined by focusing on regions of the parameter space that are most relevant to the parameter inference task.
elements.sent-2411-032: This adaptive sampling allows for more efficient exploration of the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-033: Early versions like SNPE-A \citep{papamakarios2016fast and SNPE-B \citep{NIPS2017_addfa9b7 required correction steps to account for changes in the sampling distribution, adding complexity to the process.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In contrast, Automatic Posterior Transformation (APT) or SNPE-C \citep{Greenberg19 , eliminated this need, offering a more robust and streamlined approach by leveraging normalizing flows to refine the posterior iteratively.}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, a limitation of sequential methods like APT is that they necessitate multiple rounds of training, extending the total training time.
elements.sent-2411-036: The recently proposed Jointly Amortized Neural Approximation (JANA) framework \citep{Radev23 addresses posterior and likelihood estimation through joint amortization.}
elements.sent-2411-037: JANA employs conditional invertible neural networks (cINNs) for both the posterior and likelihood networks, allowing efficient transformations between the parameter space and latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, JANA includes a trainable summary network sub-module optimized to extract maximally informative data representations in an end-to-end manner.
elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike previous approaches, JANA follows a fully amortized strategy, allowing the evaluation of normalized densities and generation of conditional random draws for parameter estimation and surrogate modeling.
elements.sent-2411-040: Simformer \citep{gloecklerall is a recent approach that leverages a probabilistic diffusion model with transformer architectures to accurately learn complex, high-dimensional posteriors, making it highly flexible.}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, the transformer-based architecture is relatively resource-intensive and involves careful optimization.
elements.sent-2411-042: We compare our proposed approach with four popular SBI methods: JANA \citep{Radev23 , GATSBI \citep{Ramesh22 , APT \citep{Greenberg19 , and NPE \citep{papamakarios2016fast .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The motivation is to cover a variety of approaches (e.g., flow-based, adversarial, one-shot, sequential) while noting that our focus is on efficiency and scalability as opposed to solely the posterior approximation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-044: We refer the reader to \citep{zammit2024neural and \citep{Radev23 for further reading on deep learning methods for SBI.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In the Bayesian framework, the aim is to infer the posterior distribution \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y ) \) over the model parameters \( \boldsymbol{\theta \), given the observed data \(\mathbf{y}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: From Bayes’ theorem, the posterior distribution can be expressed as: \begin{equation}p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) p(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{p(\mathbf{y})}, \label{eq:posterior}\end{equation} where \( p(\mathbf{y ) = \int p(\mathbf{y \mid \boldsymbol{\theta ) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta ) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta \) is the marginal likelihood, acting as a normalizing constant to ensure that the posterior is a valid probability distribution.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, as \( p(\mathbf{y ) \) does not depend upon \( \boldsymbol{\theta \), it can be ignored in the computations involving the posterior.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We propose two models for amortized inference: a Conditional Prior VAE (CP-VAE) and an Unconditional Prior VAE (UP-VAE).
elements.sent-2411-049: Both models utilize latent variables to capture hidden structures in the data.
elements.sent-2411-050: The Conditional Prior VAE uses a multivariate prior network that conditions on observed data, enabling adaptive inference that improves generalization across posterior queries.
elements.sent-2411-051: The Unconditional Prior VAE, in contrast, employs a simpler Gaussian prior with unit variance, while still capturing complex posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-052: Leveraging the variational inference framework, both models achieve competitive performance across a range of tasks, including bimodal and high-dimensional settings.
elements.sent-2411-053: The Conditional Prior Variational Autoencoder (CP-VAE) is an extension of the standard Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) \citep{NIPS2015_8d55a249,ivanov2018variational specifically designed for simulation-based inference.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We begin by introducing an auxiliary latent variable \( \mathbf{z \) to capture complex structures and dependencies within the joint distribution \(p(\boldsymbol{\theta , \mathbf{y )\) of parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\) and simulated data \(\mathbf{y}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The latent variable \(\mathbf{z}\) serves as a learned, low-dimensional summary statistic, efficiently encoding the most salient features of high-dimensional data relevant to characterize the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-056: We formulate a latent variable model \( p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \) and approximate it using a variational distribution \( q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \), parameterized by \( \psi \in \Phi \), where \( \Phi \) represents a family of distributions.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The goal is to minimize the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between this variational distribution and the true conditional distribution \citep{jordan1999introduction, kingma2022autoencodingvariationalbayes : \begin{equation}\min_{\psi \in \Phi} D_{KL}\bigg(q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \| p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})\bigg). \label{eq:KL_latent}\end{equation} Using Bayes' theorem and the chain rule of probability, the conditional distribution \(p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta )\) becomes: \begin{equation}p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{z}, \mathbf{y}) p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y})}{p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y})}. \label{eq:conditional_distribution}\end{equation} Substituting \eqref{eq:conditional_distribution into \eqref{eq:KL_latent , and denoting the KL divergence \(D_{KL (q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \| p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ))\) as \(\mathcal{D}\), we obtain: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{D} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})} \bigg[ \log q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) - \log p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}) \qquad - \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}) + \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}) \bigg]. \end{aligned} \label{eq:kl_divergence_latent}\end{equation} Rearranging the components of equation \eqref{eq:kl_divergence_latent , we isolate \(\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y )\) to derive: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}) \geq -D_{KL}\bigg(q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \parallel p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y})\bigg) + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})}\bigg[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})\bigg]. \end{aligned} \label{eq:elbo}\end{equation} This inequality stems from the fundamental non-negativity property of the KL divergence, establishing a lower bound on the log-posterior \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y ) \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

The encoder \( q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \) computes the parameters of the variational distribution for latent variables \(\mathbf{z}\), conditioned on paired simulated data \(\mathbf{y}\) and parameters \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\). The prior network \( p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}) \) defines a data-dependent prior over \(\mathbf{z}\), enforcing latent space regularization via the KL divergence. elements.sent-2411-077: To address this, we use Bayes' theorem to reformulate the conditional distribution \( p(\mathbf{z \mid \boldsymbol{\theta , \mathbf{y ) \) as: \begin{equation}p(\mathbf{z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{p(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{p(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y})}. \label{eq:bayes_conditional}\end{equation} Substituting \eqref{eq:bayes_conditional into the KL divergence formulation in \eqref{eq:KL_latent , and denoting \(D_{KL (q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \| p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ))\) as \(\mathcal{D}\), \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{D} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \bigg[\log q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}) - \log p(\mathbf{z})\bigg] \quad - \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \bigg[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{z}) - \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}) \bigg]. \end{aligned} \label{eq:kl_divergence_expanded_v3}\end{equation} Applying the chain rule to the joint probability \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta , \mathbf{y ) = p(\mathbf{y ) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y ) \) and recognizing that \( \log p(\mathbf{y ) \) is a constant with respect to \( \boldsymbol{\theta \), along with the non-negativity property of the KL divergence, we obtain: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}) \geq \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \bigg[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}) + \log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{z})\bigg] \quad - D_{KL}\bigg(q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}) \,\|\, p(\mathbf{z})\bigg). \end{aligned} \label{eq:final_inequality}\end{equation} Similar to \eqref{eq:elbo , minimizing the negative of the right-hand side of the derived inequality is equivalent to minimizing the negative log-posterior.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: The Two Moons model is a popular benchmark commonly used to evaluate the ability of SBI algorithms to recover multimodal and unconventional posterior distributions \citep{Greenberg19, lueckmann2021benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The challenge herein is to recover the bimodal nature of the posterior, and to capture its distinctive crescent shape.
elements.sent-2411-101: The parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\theta \sim \mathcal{U (-1, 1)^2\) governs the model, and the observed data \(\mathbf{x}\) is generated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{pmatrix} r \cos(a) + 0.25 r \sin(a) \end{pmatrix} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{pmatrix} -|\theta_1 + \theta_2| -(\theta_1 - \theta_2) \end{pmatrix}, \label{eq:two_moons_x}\end{equation} for radius \(r \sim \mathcal{N (0.1, 0.01^2)\) and angle \(a \sim \mathcal{U}\left(-\frac{\pi {2 , \frac{\pi {2}\right)\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Figure \ref{fig:true_posterior_2moons shows the recovered posterior using (a) CP-VAE and (b) UP-VAE model formulations.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Both models generate samples that align closely with the true posterior, with UP-VAE exhibiting slightly better coverage of the crescent shape.

elements.sent-2411-109: The Bernoulli Generalized Linear Model (GLM) simulates neuronal spiking behavior in response to a time-varying stimulus, driven by a set of covariates \citep{mechanistic_models .}
elements.sent-2411-110: With a discrete time domain of \( T = 100 \) bins, the model produces observed data in the form \(\mathbf{y = [y_i]_{i=1 ^{10}\).}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Each component \( y_i \) aggregates spiking activity across time intervals, capturing both overall spiking frequency and spatial patterns of neuronal activity.
elements.sent-2411-112: The model is parameterized by a vector \( \boldsymbol{\theta \in \mathbb{R ^{10 \), consisting of a scalar bias term \( \beta \) and an auxiliary vector \( \mathbf{f \in \mathbb{R ^{9 \): \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta \mathbf{f} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2), \quad \mathbf{f} \sim \mathcal{N}\left( \mathbf{0}, \left( \mathbf{F}^\top \mathbf{F} \right)^{-1} \right), \label{eq:glm_prior}\end{equation} where the matrix \( \mathbf{F \) enforces smoothness by penalizing second-order differences in \( \mathbf{f \), defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:matrix_F} F_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, j = i-2, -2, j = i-1, 1 + \sqrt{\frac{i-1}{9}}, j = i, 0, \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}, \quad 1 \leq i \leq 9,\end{equation} For each time bin \( i \), the binary spiking variable \( z_i \) is sampled from a Bernoulli distribution, where the spiking probability is modeled using a logistic function applied to the linear combination of covariates \( \mathbf{v _i \) and the parameter vector, expressed as: \begin{equation}z_i \sim \text{Bernoulli} \left( \sigma(\mathbf{v}_i^\top \mathbf{f} + \beta) \right), \quad \sigma(t) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-t}}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\theta =  \beta f , \quad \beta \sim N(0, 2), \quad f \sim N\left( 0, \left( F^\top F \right)^{-1} \right), \label{eq:glm_prior}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\label{eq:matrix_F} F_{ij} =  1, j = i-2, -2, j = i-1, 1 + \sqrt{\frac{i-1}{9}}, j = i, 0, otherwise. , \quad 1 \leq i \leq 9,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-113: Here, \( \mathbf{v _i \in \mathbb{R ^9 \) represents white noise inputs (covariates) drawn from \( \mathcal{N (0, \mathbf{I ) \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: The design matrix \(\mathbf{V = \left[ \mathbf{v _i \right]_{i=1 ^{T \) aggregates these inputs over time bins \( T \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: The model computes summary statistics \(\mathbf{y \in \mathbb{R ^{10}\), where the total number of spikes is represented by \(y_1 = \sum_{i=1 ^{T z_i\), and the remaining components, \(y_2, \dots, y_{10}\), are spike-triggered averages of the covariates, normalized by \(y_1\), defined as \(y_{2:10 = \frac{1 {y_1 \mathbf{V \mathbf{z}\).}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-122: The Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) model is a widely used mathematical framework in epidemiology for modeling the spread of infectious diseases within a population.
elements.sent-2411-123: This model tracks the evolution of the number of individuals in three distinct compartments: susceptible (\(S\)), infected (\(I\)), and recovered (\(R\)).
elements.sent-2411-124: The dynamics of disease spread are governed by a non-linear dynamical system characterized by two key parameters: the contact rate (\(\beta\)) and the recovery rate (\(\gamma\)).
elements.sent-2411-125: The model is described by the following set of ordinary differential equations: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt} \begin{pmatrix} S I R \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -\beta S \beta S - \gamma I \gamma I \end{pmatrix} \frac{I}{N},\end{equation} where \(N\) is the total population size.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt}  S I R  =  -\beta S \beta S - \gamma I \gamma I  \frac{I}{N},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: The parameters are sampled as: \(\beta \sim \text{LogNormal (\log(0.4), 0.5)\) and \(\gamma \sim \text{LogNormal (\log(1/8), 0.2)\).}}

elements.sent-2411-130: The results are now evaluated with respect to the following important considerations in SBI.
elements.sent-2411-131: The sequential nature of APT adds computational expense over NPE and CP/UP-VAE.
elements.sent-2411-132: Autoregressive models can be computationally expensive to train and evaluate as estimating each parameter depends on previously estimated parameters -- however, JANA is able to closely match NPE's training times for chosen hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-133: CP-VAE and UP-VAE are inherently simple, and do not require external summary statistics, aiding scalability and efficient training (Table \ref{tab:train_times ).}
elements.sent-2411-134: JANA and APT proved to be very robust, with low std.. dev.
elements.sent-2411-135: JANA is particularly suited for posteriors with dependencies between parameters.
elements.sent-2411-136: However, the flexibility of normalizing flow based approaches (NPE, APT, JANA) is hindered by the invertibility requirement.
elements.sent-2411-137: GATSBI is suitable for complex, high-dimensional problems where other approaches may struggle and learning the posterior implicitly becomes important.
elements.sent-2411-138: Both CP-VAE and UP-VAE are highly flexible and adaptable with respect to the architecture.
elements.sent-2411-139: This is of particular interest in high-dimensional problems.
elements.sent-2411-140: UP-VAE’s simple Gaussian prior \( p(\mathbf{z ) \) allows for efficient latent space sampling, easier regularization, and a consistent latent space, leading to more stable training, making it efficient and effective for simpler problems.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The proposed CP-VAE and UP-VAE models complement existing approaches by virtue of approximating the posterior distribution in a computation-efficient manner (as evidenced in Table \ref{tab:train_times ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: This makes the models well-suited for challenging problems where computational efficiency is of key consideration (e.g., large number of parameters to infer, rapid model exploration, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-143: While UP-VAE uses a simpler prior, its data decoder is computationally more expensive than the conditional prior in CP-VAE, which reflects in faster training times for CP-VAE for most problems (Table \ref{tab:train_times ).}
elements.sent-2411-144: While we note that the training times in Table \ref{tab:train_times ) can be affected by several factors including implementation and hyperparameter optimization, the comparison provides an estimate of relative computational complexity on the same hardware for reference implementations (taken from the original papers).}
elements.sent-2411-145: This paper introduces two latent variable models, CP-VAE and UP-VAE, for amortized inference in likelihood-free simulation-based inference, with a view on efficiency, scalability, interpretability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-146: The proposed models are validated against state-of-the-art normalizing flow and adversarial training-based approaches on benchmark test problems.
elements.sent-2411-147: Both CP-VAE and UP-VAE deliver comparable performance to the state-of-the-art while being highly computation efficient and scalable.
elements.sent-2411-148: Future enhancements could involve integrating more sophisticated architectures (e.g., Convolutional Neural Networks) to better capture spatial and temporal dependencies within the data.
elements.sent-2411-149: Exploring diverse prior distributions, such as truncated Gaussian priors, may further improve the models' ability to approximate complex posteriors.

elements.section-2411-007: Derivation of the Loss Function for CP-VAE Model
elements.sent-2411-152: To derive the loss function for the CP-VAE model, we begin by referencing the intractable formulation presented in Section 3.1, Equation (6) of the main text, to arrive at a tractable approximation.
elements.sent-2411-153: The original loss function is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}; \psi) = \underbrace{D_{KL}(q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \| p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}))}_{\text{Term 1}} - \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})}[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})]}_{\text{Term 2}}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:loss_v1_min}\end{equation} where, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ represents the parameter vector, $\mathbf{y}$ is the observed data, and $\psi$ consists of the parameters of the variational distribution.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation} L(\theta, y; \psi) = \underbrace{D_{KL}(q_{\psi}(z \mid y, \theta) \| p(z \mid y))}_{Term 1} - \underbrace{E_{z \sim q_{\psi}(z \mid y, \theta)}[\log p(\theta \mid y, z)]}_{Term 2},  \label{eq:loss_v1_min}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-154: To ensure tractability of the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence term, \(D_{\text{KL (q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \parallel p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y ))\), we assume both the variational distribution \(q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \sim \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _q, \boldsymbol{\Sigma _q)\) and the prior \(p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y ) \sim \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _{\mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\mathbf{y )\) to be multivariate Gaussian distributions with diagonal covariance matrices.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The diagonal structure of the covariance matrices ensures that only the variances of individual components are modeled, eliminating off-diagonal correlations.
elements.sent-2411-156: These distributions are parameterized by neural networks, with \(\boldsymbol{\mu _q\), \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma _q\), \(\boldsymbol{\mu _{\mathbf{y }\), and \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\mathbf{y }\) being functions of the network parameters \(\psi\) and \(\phi\), respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: This allows for closed-form computation of the KL divergence between the variational distribution and the prior, given by: \begin{equation}D_{KL}\left( \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{q}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}) \| \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}) \right) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \log \frac{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}|}{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}|} - d + \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}) + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q}) \right), \label{eq:kl_div_proper}\end{equation} where \( d \) represents the dimensionality of the latent variable \( \mathbf{z \).}
elements.sent-2411-158: To approximate the second term, \(\mathbb{E _{\mathbf{z \sim q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) [\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y , \mathbf{z )]\), we again assume, that \(p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y , \mathbf{z ) = \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _{\theta , \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta )\) follows a multivariate normal distribution for computational tractability, where the mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu _{\theta}\) and covariance \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta}\) are parameterized by a neural network with parameters \(\delta\).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: The probability density function of this distribution is given by: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{k/2} |\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}|^{1/2}} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta}) \right). \end{aligned} \label{eq:multi_gaussian}\end{equation} Taking the logarithm of both sides in \eqref{eq:multi_gaussian , we obtain: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}) = -\frac{1}{2} \left( k \log(2\pi) + \log |\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}| + (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta}) \right). \end{aligned} \label{eq:multi_gaussian_log}\end{equation} Since the determinant of the diagonal covariance matrix is given by \( |\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta | = \prod_{i=1 ^{k \sigma_{\theta_i ^2 \), we can simplify \( \log |\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta | \) as \( \log |\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta | = \log \left( \prod_{i=1 ^{k \sigma_{\theta_i ^2 \right) = 2 \sum_{i=1 ^{k \log \sigma_{\theta_i \).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Substituting this simplification back into \eqref{eq:multi_gaussian_log and taking the expectation with respect to \( \mathbf{z \sim q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \) on both sides, we obtain: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}}[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})] = -\frac{1}{2} \Bigg( k \log(2\pi) + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma_{\theta_i} + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \left[ (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta}) \right] \Bigg). \end{aligned} \label{eq:mean_log_loss}\end{equation} Given that \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta}\) is a diagonal matrix, the quadratic form simplifies to a sum of squares.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-161: The expectation over \(N\) data points can then be expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \left[ (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\theta}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{\theta}) \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(\theta_i - \mu_{\theta_i})^2}{\sigma_{\theta_i}^2} \right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(\theta_{i,j} - \mu_{\theta_i})^2}{\sigma_{\theta_i}^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-162: The term \(k \log(2\pi)\) is constant, and can be excluded from the optimization, allowing \eqref{eq:mean_log_loss to be written as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}}[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})] \approx - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma_{\theta_i} - \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(\theta_{i,j} - \mu_{\theta_i})^2}{\sigma_{\theta_i}^2}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:approx_log_likelihood_v1}\end{equation} Substituting \eqref{eq:kl_div_proper and \eqref{eq:approx_log_likelihood_v1 into \eqref{eq:loss_v1_min , we obtain the tractable loss function: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}; \psi,\phi,\delta) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \log \frac{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}|}{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}|} - k + \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}) + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q}) \right) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma_{\theta_i} + \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(\theta_{i,j} - \mu_{\theta_i})^2}{\sigma_{\theta_i}^2}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:final_loss_function}\end{equation} We now aim to minimize Equation \ref{eq:final_loss_function by learning the parameters $\psi$, $\phi$, and $\delta$ through stochastic gradient methods.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-163: To obtain a tractable form of the loss function for the UP-VAE model, we begin with the expression outlined in Equation (10) of Section 3.2 in the main text: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}; \psi) = \underbrace{D_{KL}(q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \| p(\mathbf{z} ))}_{\text{Term 1}} - \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})}[\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})]}_{\text{Term 2}} - \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta})}[\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{z})]}_{\text{Term 3}}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:loss_v2_min}\end{equation} We approximate Terms 2 and 3 using a formulation akin to that in Equation \(\ref{eq:approx_log_likelihood_v1}\).

elements.sent-2411-171: The Simulation-Based Inference (SBI) benchmarking test problems offer a standardized set of tasks to evaluate various inference methods.
elements.sent-2411-172: These problems span a range of dimensionalities and complexities, ensuring a comprehensive assessment across diverse scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-173: Each problem is characterized by its unique prior, simulator, and data dimensionality, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of inference methods under different conditions.
elements.sent-2411-174: Every task includes 10 observations with known ground truth posteriors and their corresponding true generating parameters.
elements.sent-2411-175: An overview of the benchmarking problems are presented in Table \ref{tab:sbi_problems .}
elements.sent-2411-176: For in-depth details about the mathematical formulations of each benchmarking test, we direct the reader to \citep{lueckmann2021benchmarking .. \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.2.}}}

elements.sent-2411-177: In both CP-VAE and UP-VAE models, we approximate the loss using a multivariate distribution with both mean and variance.
elements.sent-2411-178: Unbounded variance—where \(\sigma^2\) becomes excessively small or large—can lead to numerical instabilities, disrupting training.
elements.sent-2411-179: To improve stability, \citealp{dehaene2021reparameterizingvaesstability suggest the ExpLin parameterization, which combines exponential and linear functions: \begin{equation}\sigma(p) = \begin{cases} \exp(p) \text{if } p \leq 0, p + 1 \text{if } p > 0. \end{cases}\end{equation} This approach ensures positive variance for small values of \(p\), while preventing excessive growth for large values.}
elements.sent-2411-180: It thus balances stability and reduces the risk of training disruptions.
elements.sent-2411-181: Additionally, we use the Sigmoid-Based UpBounded parameterization in the decoder to further control variance.
elements.sent-2411-182: The UpBounded parameterization with sigmoid function is defined as: \begin{equation}\sigma(p) = \alpha + (\omega - \alpha) \cdot \text{sigmoid}(p).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-183: This formulation smoothly caps the variance within the range \([\alpha, \omega]\), ensuring controlled decoder output variance.ExpLin effectively handles the variance in the latent space, while the sigmoid-based parameterization keeps the decoder's variance stable.
elements.sent-2411-184: This approach reduces the likelihood of training disruptions and improves overall model stability.
elements.sent-2411-185: In this section, we outline the hyperparameters used for all the methods considered in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-186: We consider fixed simulation budgets of \{10,000; 20,000; 30,000\} for all test problems to evaluate various methods.
elements.sent-2411-187: The observed data and reference posterior samples come from the pre-generated datasets provided by the \codeword{sbibm library \citep{lueckmann2021benchmarking , allowing us to directly load both the observed data and the corresponding reference posterior for consistent and accurate benchmarking across different tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: An alternate implementation is also available in BayesFlow framework \citep{bayesflow_2023_software .}

elements.sent-2411-191: We tested this by generating \(10,000\) samples from the simulator using the same parameters provided in the \codeword{sbibm SIR dataset, confirming the misalignment between the observed data and the true parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-192: To address this, we considered the true parameter from \codeword{sbibm and generated the observed data by evaluating the simulator in BayesFlow.}

elements.sent-2411-254: These comparisons presented herein are general estimates primarily indicating efficiency, highlighting the trade-off between training speed and posterior inference quality.

elements.sent-2411-268: As a result, effectively inferring the parameter \( \theta \) tests the robustness of SBI methods in handling uncertainty and variability inherent in the data.

elements.sent-2411-275: Accurately estimating the underlying parameters from observed ecological time-series data requires robust SBI methods to navigate the model's stiffness and complexity.

elements.sent-2411-277: This requires robust simulation-based inference methods to address the challenges posed by highly variable and noisy infection data.

elements.sent-2411-278: In this section, we introduce the metrics used to evaluate SBI methods.
elements.sent-2411-279: We discuss how these metrics are calculated and highlight some of their potential drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-280: The Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) is a statistical method used to compare two probability distributions by training a classifier to differentiate between samples from each distribution \citep{Friedman:2003id .}
elements.sent-2411-281: This method is particularly useful in SBI for assessing the similarity between a generated posterior distribution and the true posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-282: The process of the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) involves combining samples from two distributions, \(P\) (e.g., simulated posterior) and \(Q\) (e.g., true posterior), into a single dataset, labeling the samples from \(P\) and \(Q\) as distinct classes, and training a binary classifier such as a neural network or logistic regression on this labeled dataset.
elements.sent-2411-283: The classifier's accuracy on a test set indicates the similarity between the distributions: a C2ST metric of 0.5 signifies no distinguishable difference, while a value higher than 0.5 suggests significant differences.
elements.sent-2411-284: To estimate the C2ST metric, we use the method described in the \citep{lueckmann2021benchmarking , where a two-layer neural network with ReLU non-linearity, and ten times as many units as the dimensionality of the parameters is trained.}
elements.sent-2411-285: This training involves 10,000 z-score normalized samples from both the reference and approximate posteriors, with classification accuracy reported via 5-fold cross-validation.
elements.sent-2411-286: While the C2ST metric is a powerful tool for validating simulation models, it is sensitive and can be challenging to interpret; for example, the score may reach 1 even if one dimension is poorly estimated, regardless of the accuracy in other dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-287: Additionally, the metric's outcome depends on the classifier parameters, necessitating careful methodological consideration.

elements.sent-2411-288: Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) is a statistical test used to compare two probability distributions by measuring the difference between their means in a high-dimensional feature space \citep{JMLR:v13:gretton12a .}
elements.sent-2411-289: In the context of SBI, MMD can be used to measure the similarty between the generated and the reference posteriors.
elements.sent-2411-290: Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be the two distributions being compared, and let \(\phi\) be a feature mapping function that projects the data into a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) \(\mathcal{H}\), then MMD is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{MMD}^2(P, Q; \mathcal{H}) = \left\| \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}[\phi(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{y \sim Q}[\phi(y)] \right\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2,\end{equation} where, \(\mathbb{E _{x \sim P [\phi(x)]\) is the mean embedding of the distribution \(P\), and \(\mathbb{E _{y \sim Q [\phi(y)]\) is the mean embedding of the distribution \(Q\).}}}}
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}MMD^2(P, Q; H) = \left\| E_{x \sim P}[\phi(x)] - E_{y \sim Q}[\phi(y)] \right\|_{H}^2,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-291: The norm \(\|\cdot\|_{\mathcal{H }\) measures the distance between these mean embeddings in the RKHS.}
elements.sent-2411-292: The \(\text{MMD ^2\) value quantifies the squared distance between the mean embeddings of the two distributions within the RKHS.}
elements.sent-2411-293: A \(\text{MMD ^2\) value close to zero indicates that the distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) are similar, while a larger value suggests significant differences.}

elements.sent-2411-294: Based on the results presented in the previous sections, we evaluate different methods across low-dimensional, high-dimensional, complex posterior, noisy, and time-series problems.
elements.sent-2411-295: For low-dimensional problems (e.g., Two Moons, Gaussian Mixture), CP-VAE and UP-VAE, successfully capture the true posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-296: For the Two Moons test problem, all models effectively recover the bimodal distribution and the crescent shape, except for GATSBI, which captures the bimodal structure but struggles with the intricate crescent form.
elements.sent-2411-297: In case of the Gaussian Mixture model, all methods accurately identify the shared mean of the two Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-298: Flow-based approaches perform exceptionally well in these scenarios, followed by the variational methods.
elements.sent-2411-299: As the Gaussian mixture test problem entails dependencies between parameters, JANA is particularly effective by virtue of its autoregressive nature.
elements.sent-2411-300: While GATSBI performs adequately, it misses finer details, such as complex shapes, consistent with findings reported in the original GATSBI paper \citep{Ramesh22 .}
elements.sent-2411-301: For high-dimensional problems (Gaussian Linear, Gaussian Linear Uniform, Bernoulli GLM), CP-VAE and UP-VAE consistently produce accurate posterior approximations with improved uncertainty estimates.
elements.sent-2411-302: While other methods performed well, GATSBI and JANA, despite identifying the correct regions, struggled to accurately capture the variance.
elements.sent-2411-303: As JANA entails factorization of the posterior as a series of parameter-wise conditionals, distractors hinder JANA to an extent.
elements.sent-2411-304: In complex and noisy problems (SLCP, SLCP with Distractors, Bernoulli GLM Raw), the variational models demonstrate robustness but face certain challenges.
elements.sent-2411-305: SLCP presents a complex posterior, with varying distributions across dimensions and bimodal behavior in two parameters.
elements.sent-2411-306: Both UP-VAE and CP-VAE capture the overall trends, though UP-VAE struggles particularly with dimensions exhibiting bimodal distributions.
elements.sent-2411-307: SLCP with Distractors is even more challenging, consisting of 92 uninformative and 8 informative dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-308: This complexity affects the variational methods, as CP-VAE’s prior takes the observed data as input, and noisy data impairs its learning capability.
elements.sent-2411-309: Similarly, the dual decoder structure of UP-VAE, with a reconstruction component for observed data, hampers its performance under noisy conditions.
elements.sent-2411-310: Most methods struggle with SLCP Distractors, except for APT which leverages its sequential design to better estimate the posterior.
elements.sent-2411-311: For the Bernoulli GLM Raw case, UP-VAE outperforms CP-VAE, while both GATSBI and JANA face challenges.
elements.sent-2411-312: GATSBI successfully identifies the correct region but exhibits very small variance, whereas JANA accurately infers four dimensions but struggles with the remaining dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-313: For time-series problems (Lotka-Volterra, SIR), CP-VAE proved to be a better fit than UP-VAE.
elements.sent-2411-314: The reference posterior for Lotka-Volterra has a very small variance, making accurate inference challenging for all models.
elements.sent-2411-315: JANA manages to capture all parameters accurately but still exhibits larger variance compared to the true distribution.
elements.sent-2411-316: For the SIR model, CP-VAE performs comparably to flow-based methods, while UP-VAE struggles with large variance, likely due to its dual-decoder structure that may overemphasize reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-317: GATSBI, although able to capture the overall trend, underestimates the variance, indicating a tendency to underestimate uncertainty in tight posterior regions.
elements.sent-2411-318: Sequential flow-based methods, such as APT perform well overall, but can be time-consuming due to the need for multiple rounds of inference.
elements.sent-2411-319: Flow-based methods were generally more accurate, which is expected as they directly target the exact posterior, whereas variational methods, like CP-VAE and UP-VAE, optimize a lower bound on the posterior, leading to potential underestimation of uncertainty and outliers in some cases.
elements.sent-2411-320: We also observed that VAE-based architectures tend to be more accurate and stable compared to GANs.
elements.sent-2411-321: However, managing multiple loss terms, as in UP-VAE, can be challenging, particularly with noisy data and distractors.
elements.sent-2411-322: Additionally, learning the correct prior distribution, as in CP-VAE, can be difficult because the model relies on observed data to inform the prior.
elements.sent-2411-323: If the data contains significant noise or uninformative features, the learned prior may not accurately represent the true underlying distribution, leading to suboptimal posterior approximations.
elements.sent-2411-324: Despite these challenges, VAE-based architectures still provide fast and reliable inference, particularly for high-dimensional problems.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat .}
elements.sent-2411-325: We demonstrate the ability of the proposed approach to approximate complex posteriors while maintaining computational efficiency on well-established benchmark problems.
elements.sent-2411-326: Once calibrated, the simulation model can be used to reason about, analyze and explain the observed data \(\boldsymbol{y}\) in the context of the corresponding physical process .
elements.sent-2411-327: Recent approaches such as Simformer \citep{gloecklerall combine diffusion models with transformers and cross attention to enable highly accurate posterior estimation, but entail relatively high computational cost for training/inference.}
elements.sent-2411-328: The simplicity and efficiency also makes the approach particularly suitable for heavy workflows such as model exploration, which entail repeated solution of several inference problems.
elements.sent-2411-329: A comprehensive comparison on benchmark test problems is presented in Section \ref{Experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-330: Section \ref{sec:discussion discusses the results, while Section \ref{Conclusion concludes the paper by summarizing the key findings and further advancements.}}
elements.sent-2411-331: Recent deep learning advances have spurred a variety of SBI approaches.
elements.sent-2411-332: There exist methods that focus on approximating the likelihood directly \citep{hermans2020likelihoodfree , while others concentrate on learning the posterior function \citep{papamakarios2016fast, NIPS2017_addfa9b7, Greenberg19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-333: A growing line of research pursues hybrid strategies that estimate both likelihood and posterior \citep{Radev23,glöckler2022variational,wiqvist2021sequential , aiming to balance flexibility and computational efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-334: Neural Posterior Estimation (NPE) \citep{papamakarios2016fast was one of the earliest approaches towards one-step posterior estimation, introducing the use of conditional normalizing flows to approximate the posterior distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-335: However, a limitation of sequential methods like APT is that they entail multiple rounds of training, extending the total training time.
elements.sent-2411-336: Jointly Amortized Neural Approximation (JANA) framework \citep{Radev23 addresses posterior and likelihood estimation through joint amortization.}
elements.sent-2411-337: JANA employs conditional invertible neural networks (cINNs) for both posterior and likelihood networks, allowing efficient transformations between the parameter space and latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-338: The recently proposed Simformer \citep{gloecklerall employs a score-based diffusion model combined with transformer architectures and a cross-attention conditioning mechanism, allowing it to accurately learn complex, high-dimensional posteriors with substantial flexibility.}
elements.sent-2411-339: However, diffusion-based approaches generally require iterative reverse-diffusion sampling, which, combined with the transformer’s computational overhead, results in slower amortized inference for higher-dimensional problems compared to other generative models.
elements.sent-2411-340: We compare our proposed approach with five popular SBI methods: JANA \citep{Radev23 , GATSBI \citep{Ramesh22 , APT \citep{Greenberg19 ,NPE \citep{papamakarios2016fast and Simformer \citep{gloecklerall .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-341: The motivation is to cover a variety of approaches (e.g., flow-based, adversarial, diffusion, one-shot, sequential) while noting that our focus is on efficiency and simplicity as opposed to solely the posterior approximation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-342: From Bayes’ theorem, the posterior distribution can be expressed as: \begin{equation}p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) p(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{p(\mathbf{y})}, \label{eq:posterior}\end{equation} where \( p(\mathbf{y ) = \int p(\mathbf{y \mid \boldsymbol{\theta ) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta ) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta \) is the marginal likelihood, which is a normalizing constant ensuring $p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y )$ is a valid probability distribution.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: However, as \( p(\mathbf{y ) \) is independent of \( \boldsymbol{\theta \), it can be ignored in the computations involving the posterior.}}
elements.sent-2411-344: The Conditional Prior Variational Autoencoder (CP-VAE) extends the standard Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) \citep{NIPS2015_8d55a249,ivanov2018variational specifically designed for simulation-based inference.}
elements.sent-2411-345: The loss function in \eqref{eq:optimization_problem_min is intractable due to the complexity of computing the KL divergence between the variational distribution \( q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \) and the conditional prior \( p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y ) \), as well as the expectation over the latent variable \( \mathbf{z \).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-346: To address this, we approximate \( p(\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y ) \sim \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _{\mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\mathbf{y ) \), \( q_{\psi (\mathbf{z \mid \mathbf{y , \boldsymbol{\theta ) \sim \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _{q , \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{q ) \), and \( p(\boldsymbol{\theta \mid \mathbf{y , \mathbf{z ) \sim \mathcal{N (\boldsymbol{\mu _{\theta , \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta ) \) as multivariate Gaussians with diagonal covariance matrices, where \( \boldsymbol{\mu _{\mathbf{y \), \( \boldsymbol{\mu _{q \), and \( \boldsymbol{\mu _{\theta \) are mean vectors, and \( \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\mathbf{y \), \( \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{q \), and \( \boldsymbol{\Sigma _{\theta \) are covariance matrices.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-347: Here, \( \mathbf{z \) represents the latent variable with dimensionality \( k_\mathbf{z \).}}
elements.sent-2411-348: The final loss is: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\text{CP-VAE}} = \frac{1}{2} \bigg( \log \frac{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}|}{|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}|} - k_\mathbf{z} + \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{q}) + (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{y}}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\mathbf{y}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{q}) \bigg) \quad + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \log \sigma_{\theta_i} + \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(\theta_{i,j} - \mu_{\theta_i})^2}{\sigma_{\theta_i}^2}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:final_loss_function}\end{equation} where \( k \) is the dimensionality of \( \boldsymbol{\theta \).}
elements.sent-2411-349: The model architecture is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:conditional_vae .}
elements.figure-2411-033: \includegraphics[width=5cm]{vae_twodec_1.pdf} \caption{UP-VAE architecture. The encoder \( q_{\psi}(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \) outputs the mean and variance of the approximate distribution, from which \( \mathbf{z} \) is sampled (reparameterization trick). elements.sent-2411-361: This section outlines distinctive challenges posed by each SBI benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-362: The Two Moons problem tests methods ability to capture bimodal crescent-shaped posteriors.
elements.sent-2411-363: In the Gaussian Mixture Model, the main challenge is identifying the shared mean between two Gaussian distributions with different variances.
elements.sent-2411-364: SLCP introduces complex parameter dependencies (uniform, Gaussian, multimodal, and skewed distributions), while its distractors variant adds uninformative dimensions that obscure inference.
elements.sent-2411-365: Gaussian Linear examines posterior estimation under conjugate priors, whereas Gaussian Linear Uniform extends this challenge to non-conjugate uniform priors.
elements.sent-2411-366: In Bernoulli GLM , the challenge involves estimating posteriors from Bernoulli observations with Gaussian priors with the Raw variant omitting sufficient statistics to increase complexity.
elements.sent-2411-367: The SIR epidemic model aims to infer contact and recovery rates from noisy infection data over time.
elements.sent-2411-368: Finally, the Lotka-Volterra model is challenging due to its nonlinear dynamics and potential chaotic behavior.

elements.sent-2411-382: This paper introduces two latent variable models, CP-VAE and UP-VAE, for amortized inference in likelihood-free simulation-based inference, with a view on efficiency, interpretability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-383: The study compares four generative model classes—VAEs, GANs, flow-based methods, and diffusion models—specifically tailored for simulation-based inference, evaluating them on a standardized benchmark for accuracy and training speed.
elements.sent-2411-384: Both CP-VAE and UP-VAE are able to approximate complex posterior distributions while being highly computational efficient and interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-385: Additionally, incorporating normalizing flows can provide more flexible posterior approximations, enhancing the model's ability to represent complex distributions.
elements.sent-2411-386: Exploring a variety of prior distributions, including truncated Gaussian priors, may further improve the model's capacity to approximate intricate posterior distributions.
